name: Strangford Lough Prawn Company
sustainabilityMethod: Hand-pulled creel fishing
keyFacts: Single-handed operation from one small boat, Freya
Zero seabed disturbance — creels rest on the bottom, not dragged
Near-zero bycatch — undersized prawns, egg-bearing females and non-target species returned alive
Sold direct from the harbour — no middlemen, maximum freshness
sustainabilityStory: Graeme Hackworth's creel fishing is about as low-impact as seafood gets. Twelve creels, hand-hauled, on a lough that's one of Europe's most protected marine sites. The method hasn't changed in generations — and neither has the quality.
brandVoice: Quiet, unassuming, deeply authentic. Graeme doesn't do marketing — the product does the talking. Visuals should be early morning, harbour light, wet rope, a box of langoustines.
contentIdeas: Dawn haul — go out with Graeme for a morning creel run
Side-by-side: creel-caught vs trawled langoustines — taste test
The boat Freya — a portrait of a working lough vessel

whereToBuy.label: Collection at Kircubbin Harbour or local delivery across North Down and Ards

name: Strangford Lough Oysters
sustainabilityMethod: Rope-grown aquaculture in a protected marine site
keyFacts: Grown in a designated European Marine Site and Special Area of Conservation
Each oyster filters up to 200 litres of water per day
Aquaculture actively improves water quality — removing nitrogen and phosphorus
No feed inputs — oysters filter-feed naturally from the lough
Best May through September
sustainabilityStory: Oyster farming on Strangford Lough isn't just low-impact — it actively benefits the ecosystem. The lough's exceptional water quality produces a clean, distinctively briny flavour that reflects the place entirely.
brandVoice: The lough as larder. Visuals should lean into the water, the ropes, the shells — elemental and clean. The product is the landscape made edible.
contentIdeas: How oyster farming cleans the lough — explainer with visuals
Shucking on the shore — simple fire content
Tasting notes: Strangford vs other Irish lough oysters

whereToBuy.label: Local fish markets and farm shops across County Down

name: Portavogie Prawns
sustainabilityMethod: Trawl-caught, Irish Sea, PGI-protected fishery
keyFacts: Protected Geographical Indication — one of very few PGI-protected fresh seafood products in the UK
Landed at Portavogie, one of Northern Ireland's most active fishing harbours
Same species as Strangford langoustines (Nephrops norvegicus) — different catching ground, different character
Landed and sold same-day at the harbour
sustainabilityStory: The PGI status recognises not just the quality of the catch but the specific character of the place — the Irish Sea grounds off the Ards Peninsula produce a prawn with a distinct flavour profile. The designation protects both the fishery and the community that depends on it.
brandVoice: Working harbour energy. Portavogie is a real, active fishing village — not a heritage attraction. Content should reflect the industry, the scale, the early mornings.
contentIdeas: Portavogie at dawn — the fleet going out
PGI explained: what the designation actually means for the fish and the fishermen
Comparing Portavogie prawns and Strangford langoustines — same species, different worlds

whereToBuy.label: Portavogie harbour traders, local fishmongers and markets

name: Kilkeel Fishing Industry

location.label: Kilkeel Harbour, foot of the Mournes

sustainabilityMethod: Mixed fleet with progressive MSC certification and low-bycatch gear adoption
keyFacts: Northern Ireland's largest fishing port by volume
Located at the foot of the Mourne Mountains — one of the most dramatic harbour settings on the island
Growing number of vessels MSC-certified or working towards it
Significant brown crab and mixed finfish landing volumes
Anchor of the County Down seafood economy
sustainabilityStory: Kilkeel isn't a single producer — it's a community. The harbour has been landing fish for centuries, and the industry is gradually shifting towards more selective, lower-impact gear. The story here is transition — a working port finding a more sustainable future without abandoning its identity.
brandVoice: Scale and tradition. Kilkeel is industrial in the best sense — serious, skilled, generational. Content should reflect the weight of the industry, not sanitise it.
contentIdeas: A day on the Kilkeel fleet — following a boat from the Mournes to landing
MSC certification journey — what it takes for a harbour to go sustainable
Kilkeel brown crab — landed, cooked, eaten on the harbour wall

whereToBuy.label: Wholesale, local fishmongers and harbour traders

name: Abernethy Butter
sustainabilityMethod: Hand-churned, single-farm cream — minimal processing, no industrial inputs
keyFacts: The only producers in Ireland making butter entirely by hand
Churned, washed, patted and rolled using traditional wooden butter pats
Single-farm cream sourced from the Lagan Valley
Varieties include dulse and sea salt, smoked, and black garlic
Stocked by Fortnum & Mason, Harrods, and used by Heston Blumenthal at The Fat Duck
sustainabilityStory: Abernethy is proof that the most traditional method is often the most considered one. No industrial churn, no additives — just cream, craft, and time. The single-farm sourcing means full traceability from cow to pat.
brandVoice: Quietly world-class. The Abernethy story sells itself — Harrods, The Fat Duck, hand-made in County Down. Tone should be understated confidence. The wooden pats, the texture, the craft process are the visuals.
contentIdeas: Making butter by hand — process video from churn to wrapped pat
The dulse and sea salt variety — where the seaweed comes from
County Down butter at The Fat Duck — the local-to-luxury story

whereToBuy.label: abernethybutter.com · selected delis and farm shops

name: Millbank Farm
sustainabilityMethod: Mixed farm, field-to-fork, tree planting and minimal packaging
keyFacts: Sixth-generation family farm, farming the lough shore since 1889
Own vegetables, sheep, cattle and pigs — genuinely mixed operation
On-site farm shop stocking own produce alongside local goods
Active tree planting programme and commitment to minimal packaging
Seasonal veg boxes direct from the farm
sustainabilityStory: Six generations on the same lough shore since 1889. Millbank isn't pursuing sustainability as a trend — it's how the farm has always operated. The tree planting and packaging reduction are extensions of a long-held ethic rather than recent additions.
brandVoice: Rooted, generational, quietly proud. The longevity is the story — 1889 to now, same family, same lough. Visuals should be the farm, the shore, the seasons turning.
contentIdeas: Six generations — a portrait of the family and the farm
First Comber Earlies of the season — arriving at Millbank
The view from the farm: Strangford Lough as a working landscape

whereToBuy.label: millbankfarm.com · on-site farm shop at Killinchy

name: Echlinville Distillery
sustainabilityMethod: Field-to-bottle — grain grown, milled and distilled on a single estate
keyFacts: One of the first new whiskey distilleries to open in Ireland in over a century
Grain grown on their own Ards Peninsula farm — genuinely field-to-bottle
Produces Dunville's Irish Whiskey and Jawbox Gin
Milling and distilling all on the same estate at Kircubbin
Full traceability from field to glass
sustainabilityStory: Echlinville is one of very few distilleries anywhere that can genuinely claim field-to-bottle provenance. The grain is grown on the estate, harvested, milled and distilled on site. The Ards Peninsula is in the bottle.
brandVoice: Craft with substance. Echlinville's story is about place and provenance — not lifestyle marketing. The field, the still, the bottle. Visuals: grain harvest on the peninsula, the distillery at dusk, Jawbox in context.
contentIdeas: Harvest to glass — following the grain from Ards Peninsula field to finished bottle
Jawbox Gin: the botanicals and the place they come from
The revival of Irish whiskey distilling — Echlinville's role in the story

whereToBuy.label: echlinville.com · distillery shop · local retailers

name: Copeland Distillery
sustainabilityMethod: Small-batch craft distilling with strong local provenance
keyFacts: Named for the Copeland Islands off the Down coast near Donaghadee
Small-batch production — whiskey, rum and vodka
Strong sense of local identity rooted in the County Down shoreline
One of a new generation of Northern Irish craft distillers
sustainabilityStory: Copeland's identity is inseparable from the Down coastline — the islands, the sea light, the harbour town of Donaghadee. The small-batch approach keeps production considered and the provenance genuine.
brandVoice: Coastal and contemporary. Younger energy than Echlinville — the Copeland Islands imagery gives strong visual identity. Sea, light, small batches, a harbour town.
contentIdeas: The Copeland Islands — what they are and why the distillery took their name
Donaghadee harbour at sunset with a Copeland dram
Rum from the Down coast — unexpected and worth exploring

whereToBuy.label: copelanddistillery.com · local retailers and delis

name: Comber Farmers' Market
sustainabilityMethod: Farmers' market connecting local growers directly with consumers
keyFacts: Home of the Comber Earlies — PGI-protected new potatoes unique to this area
Comber Earlies are among the first new potatoes of the Irish season each year
The rich soils around Comber produce an unusually sweet, waxy early potato
The market anchors the local artisan producer network across the Ards Peninsula
PGI status protects both the product and the growers who produce it
sustainabilityStory: The Comber Earlies PGI is one of the most tangible examples of food geography in Northern Ireland — a potato that genuinely tastes of a specific place and soil. The farmers' market keeps that connection between grower and eater direct and local.
brandVoice: Grounded and seasonal. The Comber Earlies are the star — the market is the setting. First-of-season energy: something genuinely anticipated, genuinely worth waiting for.
contentIdeas: First Comber Earlies of the year — the moment they arrive at market
What makes a Comber Early different — soil, variety, place
PGI explained through the potato: a tangible local example

whereToBuy.label: Comber town square — regular market dates throughout the year

name: Finnebrogue Artisan
sustainabilityMethod: Artisan processing, estate-reared deer and cattle, nitrite-free production
keyFacts: Pioneers of nitrite-free bacon — reformulated without preservatives now widely adopted across the industry
Red deer farmed on the Finnebrogue Estate outside Downpatrick
Wagyu beef raised on the estate — one of very few UK producers
Full traceability from estate to shelf
Stocked in major supermarkets and independent delis across the UK and Ireland
sustainabilityStory: Finnebrogue changed the bacon industry by removing nitrites — additives linked to health concerns that had been standard for decades. That instinct to challenge the conventional runs through everything they do, from deer farming to wagyu beef on a Down estate.
brandVoice: Innovation with integrity. Finnebrogue isn't a small artisan operation — it's a serious food business that happens to do things properly. The estate, the deer, the reformulation story all have weight.
contentIdeas: The nitrite-free story — how Finnebrogue changed British bacon
Deer on the Downpatrick estate — a portrait of the herd
Finnebrogue venison cooked over fire — autumn content

whereToBuy.label: finnebrogue.com · major supermarkets and delis across UK and Ireland

name: Helen's Bay Organic Gardens
sustainabilityMethod: Fully certified organic horticulture — no pesticides, no herbicides, closed fertility loop
keyFacts: Established in 1991 by John McCormick — one of Northern Ireland's organic pioneers
20-acre holding beside Belfast Lough growing over 50 varieties of vegetables
EU and IOA certified organic — licence no. 7024
Hens' manure feeds the farm's own fertility programme — a genuinely closed loop
Targets 75% local supply — currently sourcing around 65% from Northern Ireland farms
Farm shop open Fridays 10am–4pm; stall at St George's Market Belfast on Saturdays
sustainabilityStory: Since 1991, Helen's Bay Organic has been quietly proving that certified organic horticulture is viable on the North Down coast. The fertility loop — hens on site, manure back into the soil — is a model of closed-system growing. John McCormick built this before organic was a marketing term.
brandVoice: Pioneer energy, not trend energy. This is 35 years of organic conviction on the Belfast Lough shore. Visuals should be polytunnels, coastal light, the Friday farm shop, the Saturday St George's stall.
contentIdeas: 35 years organic — a portrait of John McCormick and what he built at Helen's Bay
The closed fertility loop: hens, manure, soil, vegetables
Friday farm shop: what's ready this week on the lough shore

whereToBuy.label: helensbayorganic.com · Farm shop Fridays 10am–4pm · St George's Market Belfast, Saturdays · delivery across Northern Ireland

name: Laurelbank Farm
sustainabilityMethod: Regenerative organic smallholding with Community Supported Agriculture scheme
keyFacts: 7-acre organic smallholding just outside Saintfield in County Down
Runs a Community Supported Agriculture (CSA) scheme called Farm and Feast
Grows vegetables, herbs, fruit and edible flowers across kitchen garden, field beds and polytunnels
Early 19th century farmhouse — history and purpose combined
Regular volunteer days, supper clubs, growing and cooking workshops
Mission: to reconnect people to the land through farming and feasting
sustainabilityStory: Laurelbank is one of the most community-rooted food projects in County Down. The CSA model means members are genuinely invested in the harvest — they show up, they plant, they eat what they grew. The farm and feast ethos turns food production into a shared experience rather than a transaction.
brandVoice: Warm, communal, hands-in-the-soil energy. This is the antithesis of anonymous food. The supper clubs, the volunteer mornings, the edible flower beds — all deeply photogenic and deeply human.
contentIdeas: A CSA explained through Laurelbank — what it means to be a farm member
Volunteer morning to supper club — a full day at the farm
Edible flowers from County Down — what grows and what you do with them

whereToBuy.label: laurelbankfarm.org · CSA membership and supper club events

name: Ballystockart Honey
sustainabilityMethod: Small-scale apiary beekeeping — two hives, direct to consumer, beeswax repurposed for plastic-free wraps
keyFacts: Family operation — Valerie, Frank and Finn McMullan
Two apiaries: Ballystockart (Comber) and Murlough (Dundrum, near the dunes)
Sold direct from the producer — jars from £6.50, bulk 4kg buckets available
Beeswax melted down and supplied to local producers making plastic-free food wraps
Award-winning Murlough honey — 1st place at honey show
sustainabilityStory: Two hives, two distinctive honeys from very different County Down landscapes — the farmland around Comber and the dune system at Murlough. Valerie's beeswax goes back into the local economy as plastic-free wraps, closing the loop entirely.
brandVoice: Unpretentious, local, family-run. This is beekeeping at a human scale — sold direct, priced fairly, rooted in a specific place. Murlough honey has a wildness to it that reflects the dune landscape.
contentIdeas: Two landscapes, two honeys — tasting Ballystockart vs Murlough side by side
Beeswax to wrap — following the wax from hive to plastic-free food wrap
Beekeeping in Comber: Finn's journey into the family apiary

whereToBuy.label: Direct from producer — contact via Facebook

name: Veesbees / Loughshore Apiary
sustainabilityMethod: Conservation-focused beekeeping — protecting and breeding the native Irish black bee across the Ards Peninsula
keyFacts: Established 2004 by Valentine and Chris at Loughshore Apiary, Greyabbey
Primary mission: conservation of the native Irish black bee (Apis mellifera mellifera)
Apiaries sited across the Ards Peninsula — honey reflects the local flora within a 3-mile radius of each hive
Profits support wildflower meadow introduction and native bee habitat improvement
Also produces natural beeswax skincare products
sustainabilityStory: Veesbees is as much a conservation project as a honey producer. The native Irish black bee — at home in this climate for thousands of years — is in decline across the island. Valentine and Chris have been working since 2004 to give it a foothold on the Ards Peninsula, using honey sales to fund habitat improvement.
brandVoice: Quietly purposeful. The conservation story is the lead — the honey is the means. Visuals should be the Ards landscape, wildflower meadows, the hives among the hedgerows of Greyabbey.
contentIdeas: The native Irish black bee — what it is, why it matters, and why the Ards Peninsula
Wildflower meadows and the Loughshore Apiary — habitat creation in action
Honey as conservation tool — how buying a jar funds the bees' future

whereToBuy.label: veesbees.com — direct online

name: The Honeysuckle Bee and Me
sustainabilityMethod: Native Irish honeybee keeping — unpasteurised, minimally filtered, single-area honey
keyFacts: Great Taste Award winner 2025 — one star
Honey gathered by native Irish honeybees from the flora of the Killinchy hills
Always unpasteurised and minimally filtered — retains all natural properties
Flavour profile: floral, woody and citrus notes reflecting the local landscape
Located in Killinchy — right on the Strangford Lough shore
sustainabilityStory: The hills around Killinchy — pastures, wildflowers, hedgerows, gardens — are what's in the jar. Native Irish bees, no pasteurisation, minimal filtering. A Great Taste Award in 2025 confirmed what locals already knew: this is honey that genuinely tastes of a specific place.
brandVoice: Precise and place-specific. The Killinchy landscape — the same lough shore as Millbank Farm — is the identity. Great Taste recognition gives credibility without losing the small-scale authenticity.
contentIdeas: What does Killinchy taste like? — tasting notes and the landscape behind them
Native Irish bees on the lough shore — a portrait of the hives at Killinchy

name: Hillsborough Honey (BT26)
sustainabilityMethod: Small apiary beekeeping — unprocessed, unheated, unblended honey from BT26 hives
keyFacts: All honey from hives kept exclusively in the BT26 (Hillsborough) area
Completely unprocessed — not heated, blended, homogenised or treated
Award-winning cut comb honey — multiple 1st prizes at the Bristol Honey Show
Also produces award-winning cut comb straight from the hive
Available direct from the producer
sustainabilityStory: Honey that is entirely what it says — unprocessed, unblended, from a specific postcode. The cut comb is taken straight from the hive, honeycomb and all. Multiple show prizes reflect a beekeeper who takes quality seriously at every stage.
brandVoice: Precise and uncompromising about quality. The postcode specificity — BT26 — is an interesting hook. This is genuinely local honey in the most literal sense.
contentIdeas: What unprocessed really means — comparing raw vs supermarket honey
Cut comb explained — honey straight from the hive, wax and all

name: Michael Young MBE — Hillsborough Honey
sustainabilityMethod: Expert small-scale apiary beekeeping — natural honey, comb honey and bee pollen from Hillsborough hives
keyFacts: MBE awarded in 2009 for voluntary services to beekeeping
Founder of the Institute of Northern Ireland Beekeepers (INIB)
International senior honey judge — one of the most respected figures in Irish and UK beekeeping
Produces natural honey, comb honey, raw bee pollen and mead
Stocked at Coleman's Garden Centre and In Klöver, Hillsborough village
sustainabilityStory: Michael Young is the closest thing Northern Ireland beekeeping has to a founding figure. He built the Institute of Northern Ireland Beekeepers from scratch, judges honey internationally, and still keeps his own hives in Hillsborough. His honey carries the weight of genuine expertise — produced by someone who has spent decades understanding exactly what makes honey exceptional.
brandVoice: Authoritative but not showy. The MBE, the INIB, the international judging — this is someone who has given beekeeping to Northern Ireland as much as he has taken from it. The story is as much about knowledge and craft as it is about a jar of honey.
contentIdeas: The man who built Northern Irish beekeeping — a portrait of Michael Young

whereToBuy.label: In Klöver, Hillsborough · Coleman's Garden Centre farm shop

name: The Cookie Jar Bakery
sustainabilityMethod: Traditional craft baking — unchanged family recipes, local ingredients, three generations of knowledge
keyFacts: Family-owned craft bakery since 1965, now in its third generation
Founded by James and Jean Herron — James trained at Loughry College and Glasgow College of Food Technology
Famous for wheaten bread — every recipe unchanged since grandmother Jean's originals
Two shops in Newcastle plus a location in Kilkeel
Also produces award-winning take-home bread mixes — soda, wheaten and scone — shipped worldwide to ex-pats
sustainabilityStory: Three generations of the same family, the same recipes, at the foot of the Mourne Mountains since 1965. The Cookie Jar is a living archive of County Down baking — wheaten bread and fruit bannock made exactly as Jean Herron made them in the 1960s. The bread mix range means the taste of Newcastle travels to ex-pats in Australia, America and Europe.
brandVoice: Unhurried, deeply rooted, Mourne-framed. This is bread that people travel for and take home in their luggage. The family continuity story — grandmother to father to James — is the identity.
contentIdeas: Jean's recipe, sixty years on — the story of the wheaten bread that didn't change
Baking at the foot of the Mournes — a morning in the Cookie Jar kitchen
The bread mix that crosses oceans — ex-pat demand and what it says about Northern Irish food identity

whereToBuy.label: 121 Main Street, Newcastle · The Market Square, Kilkeel

name: The Heatherlea Bakery
sustainabilityMethod: Traditional Northern Irish craft baking with natural local ingredients — no industrial shortcuts
keyFacts: Established 1937 — owned by Paul and Patricia Getty since 1990
Four-time NI Bakery of the Year — most recently 2023
Multiple Great Taste Awards including two gold stars for Coarse Wheaten Bread
Team of 40 bakers with a combined 150 years of experience
Classic Irish soda, potato and wheaten alongside spelt, multiseed and speciality loaves
Wholesale to independent retailers across Northern Ireland
sustainabilityStory: The Heatherlea has been baking in Bangor since 1937 — through rationing, the decline of the bread van era, and the revival of artisan food culture. Paul and Patricia Getty have stewarded it since 1990 with handwritten recipe books from their predecessors still in regular use. The bread is taken by visitors as far as Australia and Canada as a taste of home.
brandVoice: Bangor's bakery — earned, not claimed. The awards, the longevity, the regular customers who have been coming for decades. Traditional meets contemporary is their own framing — and it's accurate. Tone should be warm and confident.
contentIdeas: The handwritten recipe books — baking heritage passed down through Bangor kitchens

name: Counter Culture Bakery
sustainabilityMethod: Real sourdough — flour, water and salt only. Wild yeast starter, no additives, no shortcuts
keyFacts: Sean Kelly's sourdough micro-bakery based in Bangor
Pure ingredients: flour, water and salt — nothing else
Sourdough crafted over two days using a wild yeast starter dating to 2016
Stocked at Millbank Farm Shop, Indie Fude, Lightfoot, Ashvale Farm Shop and local markets
Open Mon, Wed, Thu, Fri, Sat 8am–2pm at Unit 71, 2–4 Balloo Avenue, Bangor
sustainabilityStory: Counter Culture is as minimal as bread gets — flour, water, salt, time, and a starter that's been alive since 2016. Sean Kelly's approach sits at the opposite end of the spectrum from industrial baking. The fact that his bread is stocked at Millbank Farm Shop makes it a natural fit for this guide.
brandVoice: Principled and pared back. The name says it: this is bread as a rejection of industrial shortcuts. Young, local, rooted in craft. Instagram presence (@counterculturerealbread) reflects the aesthetic well.

name: Knead Bakery
sustainabilityMethod: Father-to-son artisan baking — everything prepared fresh on-site daily using local ingredients
keyFacts: 24 years on the High Street in Newtownards
Family-run — expertise passed from father to son through generations
Everything prepared fresh every morning on-site
Full range of traditional and artisan baked goods
Vegetarian and vegan options available
sustainabilityStory: Knead has been baking fresh every morning in Newtownards for over two decades. The father-to-son tradition keeps craft knowledge alive in a town centre that benefits from a genuine independent bakery on its high street.
brandVoice: Steady, local, reliable. Twenty-four years on the same street is itself a statement. The generational skill transfer is the story — old knowledge in a contemporary bakery.

name: Whitewater Brewery
sustainabilityMethod: Fifth-generation family farm brewery — local ingredients, mountain water, independent production
keyFacts: Founded 1996 on a 5th generation family farm on the banks of the Whitewater river
Largest microbrewery in Northern Ireland
All beers brewed with naturally soft water from the Mourne Mountains
Multiple Great Taste Awards and international recognition
On-site taproom with brewery tours available
sustainabilityStory: Whitewater started in a barn before the craft beer movement existed in Ireland. Bernard and Kerry Sloan built it on their family farm in Castlewellan — five generations of farming on the same land, with the river running past the door. The water from the Mournes is the ingredient that ties everything together.
brandVoice: Rooted, pioneering, unpretentious. This is the original County Down craft brewery — predating the trend by years. The family farm setting and the taproom are both strong content opportunities.
contentIdeas: Born in a barn — the story of Northern Ireland's original craft brewery
Mountain water, mountain beer — the Spelga Dam connection

name: Mourne Mountains Brewery
sustainabilityMethod: Independent microbrewery — Irish base malts, house-cultivated yeast, Mourne mountain water
keyFacts: Founded 2015, inspired by a passion for craft brewing
All beers brewed with naturally soft water from Spelga Dam in the Mourne Mountains
Base malts from trusted Irish suppliers — house yeast cultivated in Ireland
All beers vegan-friendly
Multiple Great Taste Awards including two gold stars for Mourne Mist Pilsner and East Coast IPA
Wide range from crisp pilsners to bold imperial stouts — small batch seasonal releases
sustainabilityStory: Mourne Mountains Brewery takes its identity entirely from the landscape — the water from Spelga Dam is what makes the beer. Founded in 2015, it's become a major part of Northern Ireland's craft beer scene while staying true to its County Down roots.
brandVoice: Bold and landscape-driven. The Mournes are the brand — every beer name and label connects back to the mountains. Strong visual identity with genuine provenance to back it up.

name: Peninsula Kelp Company
sustainabilityMethod: Hand-dived seaweed harvesting under Crown Estate licence — rotational cropping, no machinery
keyFacts: Founded 2019 by Andrea and Graham Gannon — he is a commercial diver, she drives product development
Holds Crown Estate licence to hand-harvest marine products from County Down waters
Rotational cropping plan protects the seabed — no machinery used
Products include dulse crackers, pure dried kelp, kelp popcorn, infused oils, seaweed butter and seaweed coffee
Supplies Noble Isle and exports globally — US, Canada, New Zealand, France and beyond
The whole family dives — including their two daughters
sustainabilityStory: Graham dives up to 5 metres to harvest kelp and dulse by hand from the waters around Strangford Lough, towing his haul back to shore in a bag and float. The Crown Estate licence and rotational cropping plan mean the seabed recovers between harvests. Andrea transforms the raw seaweed into a range of products that are genuinely hard to find anywhere else in Northern Ireland.
brandVoice: Genuinely adventurous — the diving, the wetsuit, the early morning sea swims. But also domestic and approachable: seaweed butter, dulse crackers, kelp popcorn. The family dimension (kids diving too) is warm and unusual.
contentIdeas: A morning harvest — diving for kelp in Strangford Lough with Graham
Seaweed to butter — the transformation from lough floor to Abernethy collaboration
Peninsula Kelp coffee: the most unexpected product from the lough

name: Mike's Fancy Cheese — Young Buck
sustainabilityMethod: Small-batch raw milk cheesemaking — single herd sourcing, hand-ladled, traditional methods
keyFacts: Young Buck: Northern Ireland's first and only raw milk blue cheese, launched November 2013
Made from milk from a single herd of 150 Holstein Friesian cows near Newtownards
Recipe based on Practical Cheesemaking, a book published in 1917
Hand-ladled into moulds — aged minimum 12 weeks
Best Blue Cheese in Ireland, 2024 Irish Cheese Awards
Stocked at Neal's Yard Dairy, Sheridans, and top restaurants across Ireland
sustainabilityStory: Michael Thomson left a career in social work, worked in a Belfast deli, studied at the School of Artisan Food and came home to Newtownards to make Northern Ireland's first raw milk cheese. Young Buck is built on a 1917 recipe, single-herd milk, and a set of values about what cheese should be. The 2024 Irish Cheese Award for Best Blue is the latest in a decade of recognition.
brandVoice: Principled, confident, quietly iconoclastic. Mike's is doing something genuinely novel in Northern Ireland — raw milk cheese — and doing it at the highest level. The origin story (social worker to cheesemaker) is compelling.
contentIdeas: From the herd to the cave — tracing a wheel of Young Buck from milk to maturity
Raw milk explained — why it matters and what Mike's does differently
Young Buck at 1 month, 2 months, 4 months — a tasting through the ageing process

name: Leggygowan Farm
sustainabilityMethod: Single-farm goat's cheese — milk from own 100-strong pedigree herd, handcrafted on the 20-acre farm
keyFacts: Established 2010 by brothers Adam, Ryan and Jason Kelly as a farm diversification project
100-strong herd of pedigree goats including Nubians on a 20-acre farm at Saintfield
Produced Northern Ireland's first goat's blue cheese in 2012
Bronze medal, International Cheese Festival (Nantwich) 2012
Supplies leading hotels, restaurants and delis across Northern Ireland
sustainabilityStory: Three brothers, one farm, an unexpected pivot into goat's cheese — and Northern Ireland's first goat's blue cheese as the result. Leggygowan is a genuine farm diversification success story, using milk entirely from their own herd on a farm outside Saintfield.
brandVoice: Three brothers, 100 goats, one County Down farm. The diversification story is real and relatable. The goat's blue is the lead product — unusual, locally significant, well-awarded.
contentIdeas: Northern Ireland's first goat's blue — the story of the Leggygowan breakthrough
Meet the herd — pedigree Nubians on a Saintfield farm
Three brothers and a cheesemaking dream — a family portrait

name: Finlay's Place
sustainabilityMethod: Pick-your-own soft fruit — direct from grower, zero food miles, seasonal harvest
keyFacts: Family-run soft fruit farm established 2011 at Killinchy on the Strangford Lough shore
Pick-your-own raspberries, gooseberries, blackcurrants and redcurrants
Also sells potatoes, eggs and vegetables direct
Georgian farmhouse setting — Finlay returned to the house where he was born after 32 years in Warwickshire
Right on the Comber Road — lough-shore location
sustainabilityStory: Finlay returned to the house where he was born on the Strangford Lough shore after three decades away, and planted soft fruit. The PYO model means customers do the picking themselves — zero packaging, zero food miles, maximum connection to where food comes from.
brandVoice: Quietly personal and place-rooted. The return-home story gives it depth. The lough-shore setting links it directly to the wider Strangford identity. PYO soft fruit is a strong summer content opportunity.
contentIdeas: Coming home — Finlay's return to the lough shore and what he planted
PYO on the Strangford shore — summer soft fruit picking at Killinchy
Blackcurrants and raspberries from the lough — what to make with them

name: Hilden Brewery
sustainabilityMethod: Independent family brewery — over 40 years of craft production in a historic Georgian setting
keyFacts: Founded 1981 — the oldest independent brewery on the island of Ireland
Set in the Georgian courtyard of Hilden House, a former Barbour linen company estate
Thomas Andrews, designer of the Titanic, was married at Hilden House
Two brewhouses, a bottling line and a canning line added in recent years
Brewery tours available with beer flight and traditional Irish stew
On-site Tap Room restaurant and Threads café
sustainabilityStory: Over 40 years of independent brewing in the same Georgian courtyard — Hilden has been doing this since before craft beer was a concept in Ireland. The brewery sits inside a piece of Irish linen and industrial history, and has quietly outlasted every trend while continuing to make honest, well-crafted beer.
brandVoice: History and character in equal measure. The oldest independent brewery in Ireland, in a Georgian linen house, where the designer of the Titanic got married. That's a lot of story in one courtyard. Tone should be warm and understated — this place doesn't need to shout.
contentIdeas: 40 years in the same courtyard — the story of Ireland's oldest independent brewery
Hilden House and the Barbour linen legacy — what came before the brewery
The Titanic connection — Thomas Andrews, Hilden House, and a pint of Headless Dog
